# UC-19.1.2 · Service Profile Compliance (Cisco UCS)

> **Criticality:** High &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Observability &middot; **Type:** Configuration, Compliance &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We keep a master list of the server “recipes” your team defines, and we check whether each recipe is really painted onto the right machine, with no half-finished or broken paint jobs. We flag when a recipe changed but the machine is still running the old settings, or when the job failed, so the apps never wake up to the wrong network identity on the next boot.*

---

## Description

Classifies `lsServer` (Service Profile) rows from `cisco:ucs:inventory`, after dropping `lsServer` **templates** via `type` values that end in `template`, by mapping normalized `assocState` and `configState` pairs into Compliant, PendingReboot, ApplyFailed, Drifted, Transitional, Unassociated, Associating, AssocFailed, or Unknown. The `stats` stage keeps the latest `pnDn`, `srcTemplName`, and poll time per `dn` and org path, without filtering on a synthetic `object_type` and without the non-canonical `cisco:ucs:config` sourcetype.

## Value

A Service Profile is the server’s identity: boot policy, vNICs, and firmware policy. When the profile is not fully applied, pending reboot, or has failed the push, the next restart or a quiet blade swap is when hypervisors, clusters, and bare-metal applications discover the wrong identity. Tracking these states in Splunk gives the platform team early warning, supports change-control evidence (who was supposed to be on which template, and was it really applied), and helps capacity owners spot profiles sitting unassociated in pools instead of on revenue-bearing hosts.

## Implementation

Enable the Cisco UCS **inventory** modular input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` so `cisco:ucs:inventory` is populated, then schedule the `spl` weekly for a posture report and, if desired, a 15-minute alert restricted to `ApplyFailed`, `Drifted`, and `AssocFailed` with throttle keys on `dn` and `compliance`. Pair the alert body with a saved search over `cisco:ucs:audit` for the same time window so the first responder can name the change event that created the wave.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Splunk Add-on for Cisco UCS (`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`, Splunkbase 2731, v4.3.1 or newer) installed on a Heavy Forwarder or any host permitted to run modular inputs, plus the same add-on (or a lightweight TA copy) on Search Heads for saved searches, lookups, and field extraction.
- A dedicated UCSM read-only service account with API rights to the organisation (`org`) and `ls` (Service Profile) subtrees so the poller can resolve **lsServer** objects. Do not use personal admin credentials. Store credentials in the Splunk password store or protect `local/inputs.conf` with host ACLs; rotate on your standard API cadence.
- Network: outbound 443/tcp from the collection host to each UCSM cluster virtual IP (or FQDN) without TLS forward-proxy break; import the full UCSM certificate chain if you terminate on an internal CA.
- Index: typically `cisco_ucs` — align the modular input’s destination index with the one your `srchIndexesAllowed` role can search.
- License: inventory poll volume is moderate versus wire-speed `cisco:ucs:stats`, but a large org tree and a short interval still add up — sample License Usage for one day before you set sub-5-minute alert schedules everywhere.
- Scope: this use case is for UCSM-managed B-series, M-series, and C-series that register Service Profiles. Intersight-led inventory for X-class compute has different fields and a different add-on; do not point auditors at this search for those assets.

MO and field vocabulary (read this before the numbered steps): a Service Profile in UCSM is the managed object class **lsServer**. Its `dn` sits under `org-root/.../ls-...`. The `assocState` and `configState` fields are small UCSM enums, not free text: they describe the association to a physical node (`associated`, `unassociated`, `establishing`, and failure classes such as `failed`) and the push state of the policy bundle (`applied`, `not-applied`, `pending-reboot`, `failed-to-apply`, `applying`, `unsupported-config`). The `pnDn` field is the physical node DN (blade or rack) when a profile is associated. The `srcTemplName` field points at the source template, if the profile was derived from one. The `type` field separates a bound instance from a template object (`initial-template` or `updating-template`), which you exclude from the server-compliance pass in the base SPL.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection and the baseline**
(1) Install `Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs` and open Splunk Web → **Settings → Data inputs**; locate the **Cisco UCS** inventory modular input family (naming is versioned—mirror the `default/inputs.conf` template into `local/inputs.conf`, set `disabled = 0`, set the UCSM URL, credentials, and the destination index, usually `cisco_ucs`). Keep **sourcetype** as the add-on’s inventory tag: `cisco:ucs:inventory`. The sourcetype `cisco:ucs:config` does not appear in the canonical add-on list and will not return Service Profile rows at customer sites; if you see it in an old runbook, replace it.
(2) Confirm inventory includes **lsServer** poll classes — not just firmware or equipment snapshots. The exact class list is governed by the add-on version; the proof is in the probe, not a slide deck.
(3) Run discovery, in order: `index=cisco_ucs | stats count by sourcetype` (expect `cisco:ucs:inventory`), then `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" | stats count by classId | sort - count` (you want `lsServer` with non-zero count), then `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" | search classId=lsServer | head 5 | table _raw, classId, dn, assocState, configState, pnDn, srcTemplName, type` to see the field spellings your TA build actually uses, and a wildcard pass `| search classId=lsServer | head 5 | table _raw, classId, dn, *State*` in case the TA renamed state columns. If a field is missing, extend your `coalesce()` in the `spl` field—never invent `object_type` for filtering.
(4) Optional **baseline** lookup (for orgs that require a declared “gold” template per app tier): add `ucs_service_profile_baseline.csv` to your private app, UTF-8, with at least: `org` (string prefix as in the org path), `name_pattern` (glob for profile short names, you can join in SPL with `searchmatch` or a `lookup` that uses a wildcard if you pre-expand), `expected_template`, `expected_assoc` (for example `associated` or a placeholder `any`), and `notes`. In `transforms.conf` in the same app:
```
[ucs_service_profile_baseline]
filename = ucs_service_profile_baseline.csv
```
Reload the app bundle. Join this lookup in a **separate** panel if you need “declared template must equal `srcTemplName` for tier X”; the main `spl` in this use case is live-state class-based compliance, not spreadsheet-only matching.

**Step 2 — Create the search, report, and alert**
Paste the `spl` field from this use case. Stage-by-stage meaning: the base search **scopes** 24h of `cisco:ucs:inventory` so you see a recent snapshot per profile. `classId="lsServer"` is the true TA filter, not a fictional `object_type`. The `where` that rejects `*template` **types** keeps `initial-template` and `updating-template` out of a server-association pass—those are still `lsServer` objects in the API, but they never associate. `coalesce` blocks absorb harmless naming drift between `assocState` and `assoc_state` style fields. The **case** maps UCSM’s paired enums into operational names: `Compliant` is the only steady green; `ApplyFailed` and `Drifted` are the usual paging drivers; `PendingReboot` and `Transitional` are “watch on the dashboard, not 03:00 unless policy says so”; `Unassociated` is often capacity, not a defect, until your CMDB said that profile should own a host. The **stats** block collapses to one row per `org+name+dn+compliance` in the lookback, carrying the most recent `pnDn` and template name and the latest poll time. `days_since_seen` highlights silent feeds. **Alerting** (suggested): schedule a weekly saved search and export; schedule a 15-min alert for `compliance IN ("ApplyFailed","Drifted","AssocFailed")` with a **suppress** of 2–4 hours on `dn+compliance` to survive maintenance churn. Throttle, do not turn off, `PendingReboot` if your org treats yellow as normal during template pushes—use the campaign lookup instead.

**Step 3 — Validate against Cisco UCS Manager**
Copy one row’s `dn` from the Splunk table. In UCSM, open the **Servers** area and go to **Service Profiles**; navigate the org tree so the left-hand path matches the `org-root/.../ls-...` chain in `dn` (4.x: expand **root** and each **org-*** segment until the named profile). Open that profile. On the **Status**-style summary, compare **assocState** and **configState** to what Splunk printed after lowercasing: they should match the UCSM GUI, modulo poll delay. If the status is `pending-reboot`, open the **Pending Activities** or FSM work queue for the profile and read the one-line description your change manager should attach to the ticket. If the state is `failed-to-apply`, use the FSM (Finite State Machine) tab on the **lsServer** object in Advanced or the profile’s FSM sub-view in your UCSM build—copy the step name and the fault code, not a paraphrase, into the incident. If Splunk and UCSM differ for more than one inventory cycle, re-check you are in the right UCSM cluster FQDN and the API user’s org scope: GUI admins often test with broader rights than the Splunk read-only account.

**Step 4 — Operationalise**
Build a “Service profile posture” dashboard with the `visualization` set: compliance mix, exception table, and org×state matrix. **RACI**: the **compute platform** team owns the **lsServer** state in UCSM; **application** owners are pulled in when `srcTemplName` maps through CMDB to a revenue service that is in `Drifted` or `ApplyFailed` on a host that should be in production. **Change** integration: for template campaigns, add a `maintenance` or `template_campaign` lookup keyed on `srcTemplName` and the ISO date window, and only auto-close yellow rows when the change record number is in the lookup row. **Evidence**: export weekly CSVs of the table to the restricted share or ticketing attachment your **SOX/ISO** process already uses; the inventory search is a reasonable attachment for “we know which profile was not fully applied in week N.”

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
- **Zero `cisco:ucs:inventory` events** — input disabled, wrong index, 401/403, TLS, or the Heavy Forwarder cannot reach the UCSM VIP. Read `$SPLUNK_HOME/var/log/splunk/splunkd.log` for the add-on’s python class name, then `curl -vk` from the same host. Fix the trust store before you tune SPL.
- **Events exist, but `classId=lsServer` is empty** — your poll set does not include that MO class, or a site-specific transform dropped `classId`. Re-read `default/inputs.conf` in the add-on and compare with a raw `| head` from inventory without filters.
- **Every profile shows `Transitional` or `Unknown`** — field names from your TA are not the ones in `coalesce()`. Rerun the Step 1 probe and add the new spellings to the `eval` lines.
- **Hundreds of `Compliant` disappear after an upgrade** — an RBAC or org-scope change on the read-only user: permissions story, not a mass drift event.
- **All rows `Unassociated` in a lab org** — you are polling a lab tree with no server pool; that is an exclusion filter, not a product bug.
- **Templates appear in the compliance table** — your `type` field is null or a non-standard value; re-check the `!match(type,"template$")` guard and, if the TA only sets `type` for certain sites, add an explicit `search NOT (classId=lsServer AND (like(dn, "%/ls-%") ...))` after confirming template `dn` patterns in five samples.
- **`last_seen` is ancient even though the GUI changed minutes ago** — poll interval, Heavy Forwarder clock skew, or the wrong UCSM peer in a split-brain work-around; compare against `_indextime`.
- **FSM in UCSM is green but Splunk still says `not-applied`** — wait for the next **inventory** cycle; the **stats** feed is not the FSM. If the gap is hours, the inventory class list is still wrong.
- **A whole cluster went quiet after a migration to Intersight** — the authoritative profile stream moved; this UCSM `lsServer` count dropping to near zero is a data-source flag, not proof that the physical servers lost profiles.
- **You never know who changed the template** — you have inventory only. Enable and index `cisco:ucs:audit` on the same index or a parallel security index and link searches by time and `dn` prefix.
- **`AssocFailed` storms during UCSM version upgrades** — treat as a known vendor maintenance window, filter with a calendar lookup, and still verify in UCSM that the cluster is healthy before muting a brand-new class of error codes.

## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
| search classId="lsServer"
| where !match(coalesce(type, ""), "template$")
| eval org=if(match(dn, "/ls-"), replace(dn, "^org-root/(.+)/ls-[^/]+$", "\1"), "unparsed")
| eval name=coalesce(name, "")
| eval assoc=lower(coalesce(assocState, assoc_state, ""))
| eval confg=lower(coalesce(configState, config_state, ""))
| eval pn=coalesce(pnDn, pn_dn, "")
| eval compliance=case(
    assoc="associated" AND confg="applied", "Compliant",
    assoc="associated" AND confg="pending-reboot", "PendingReboot",
    assoc="associated" AND confg="failed-to-apply", "ApplyFailed",
    assoc="associated" AND confg="not-applied", "Drifted",
    assoc="associated" AND (confg="applying" OR confg="unsupported-config"), "Transitional",
    assoc="unassociated", "Unassociated",
    assoc="establishing", "Associating",
    assoc="failed" OR assoc="throttled" OR assoc="removing", "AssocFailed",
    1=1, "Unknown"
  )
| stats max(_time) as last_seen, latest(eval(if(pn!="",pn,null()))) as physical_dn, latest(eval(coalesce(srcTemplName, src_templ_name, ""))) as template, latest(coalesce(type, "")) as profile_type by org, name, dn, compliance
| eval days_since_seen=round((now()-last_seen)/86400, 1)
| sort 0 - days_since_seen compliance org name
```

## Visualization

(1) Pie or column chart of `compliance` to show the mix of Compliant, PendingReboot, Drifted, and failure-class buckets. (2) Table of non-Compliant rows with `org`, `name`, `template`, `physical_dn`, `days_since_seen`, and `dn` for copy-paste into UCSM. (3) **Single value** of Compliant share in the 24h window, plus a second tile for `ApplyFailed` count. (4) Status matrix: sub-organizations (from the org path) by compliance for backlog ownership. (5) **Timechart** of daily `Drifted` and `PendingReboot` rows during a template roll-out, with a maintenance-lookup campaign overlay for context.

## Known False Positives

**Updating-template edits** that intentionally create a `pending-reboot` wave across every child profile are a positive finding that already has a change ticket: mute the alert with a lookup keyed on `srcTemplName` and the approved campaign window, not by ignoring all yellow rows. **Planned blade or rack service** often shows a few poll cycles of `unassociated` or `establishing` before the profile re-homes: require the bad state in two or more inventory polls, or a minimum dwell time, before paging. **Audit-only or lab org trees** that never have compute attached keep `unassociated` profiles forever: exclude those org DNs with an allow-list, not with wishful `NOT compliance=*` noise filters. **RBAC** on the read-only user the modular input uses can hide entire org subtrees; Splunk will look like profiles vanished when the GUI admin still sees them—this is a credential-scope defect, not profile drift, until the API user matches the org being audited. **Incompatible firmware or policy pairs** you already triaged in UCSM may leave `failed-to-apply` until a fix ships; use a `code` or policy name lookup to suppress known, ticketed stragglers. **Initial-template-derived profiles** that were customised after binding still show an old `srcTemplName`; divergence is often intentional, so the compliance row is about state enums, not template-name equality, unless you add a stricter join to your own CMDB. **Clock skew and replication** between UCSM, the forwarder, and the indexer can make `last_seen` look a day old even when the poll succeeded—compare `_indextime` to `_time` for the same `dn` before you declare a dead feed. **UCSM high-availability failover** in the same minute as the poll can produce one odd `assocState` read; reconcile on the next cycle before a duplicate incident.
